name: service-accounts
description: Machine credentials for inter-service authentication. Issues HS256-signed JWTs that carry service-to-service call authorization. Each ServiceAccount is bound to one Application via `application_id` FK; optionally tenant-scoped for tenant-specific integrations (webhook signers, tenant AI service endpoints, etc.). Ships two credential mechanisms: (a) direct secret hash — a bcrypt-hashed rotation secret used as an HTTP Basic-like credential at the token-exchange endpoint, (b) HS256 JWT signing per-account — each ServiceAccount holds its OWN `signer_kid` linking to the auth module's `jwt_signing_keys` table for revocation isolation. Mandatory secret rotation via `expires_at` column (default 90-day TTL, 14-day warning window). Never used for interactive login — no MFA, no password change, no session lifecycle. NEVER interactive: no MFA, no email verification, no user-facing endpoints. Every request from a service account carries `X-Service-Account-Id` header + the HS256 JWT; downstream services verify the JWT signature (via JWKS or the shared secret keyed by kid) + confirm the SA is enabled + confirm the requested tenant scope (if any). Ships the `service_account.audit` middleware that heavy-audits every request (SOC 2 CC6.1 — every machine action correlated to its identity). Ships eight lifecycle events, five queued notifications, four maintenance jobs, nine Artisan commands, two validation rules, three request-time middlewares, and one platform-plane HTTP surface (`/api/v1/platform/service-accounts/*`). Plus one unauthenticated exchange endpoint (`POST /api/v1/service-accounts/token`) that swaps a bcrypt secret for a short-lived JWT. Wave 1a: HS256 signing with per-tenant shared secrets is the baseline; Wave 1c introduces RS256 asymmetric signing alongside — the same signer_kid column migrates without shape change.

packages: laravel/framework (Hash facade wrapped via SecretRotator for bcrypt rotation-secret hashing)
firebase/php-jwt (composed via auth::JwtSigner — this module never touches the JWT library directly)
wraps.rationale: We do NOT sign JWTs directly — `ServiceAccountJwtIssuer` bridges to the `auth::JwtSigner` service (owned by the `identity/auth` module, priority 25) with the per-SA signing key resolved from the `jwt_signing_keys` table by `signer_kid`. The `signer_kid` column on this module's rows names the KEY, not the JWT. Every ServiceAccount holds its own kid so revocation is isolated per-account. Password-hash rotation for the exchange secret runs through `SecretRotator` which wraps the Laravel Hash facade with bcrypt at cost 12.

platform-admin.description: Platform-plane CRUD for machine credentials. `platform_admin` guard exclusively. `super_admin`-scoped for creation + rotation + deletion; `security` + `ops` can read + issue test JWTs; every write writes a heavy audit row via `service_account.audit` middleware.

public-exchange.description: Unauthenticated exchange endpoint used by service accounts to trade their bcrypt-hashed secret for a short-lived HS256 JWT. Aggressively rate-limited (per-SA per-IP). Never exposes SA existence — unknown SA + wrong secret return the same generic error to avoid enumeration.

compliance_note: SOC 2 CC6.1 (logical access controls — every machine action correlated to its identity via service_account.audit middleware) + CC7.2 (system monitoring — dormant credential detection + suspicious-activity alerts) + NIST SP 800-131A (minimum HS256 key length 32 bytes; enforced by the auth module's JwtSigningKey creation flow, not this module) + OWASP ASVS V6 (service account key rotation, revocation, and audit trail).
tenancy_note: One of the eight rows carrying `application_id` DIRECTLY (per `.kiro/steering/tenancy-columns.md` §2). Additionally optionally carries `tenant_id` — non-null = tenant-scoped service (webhook signer, tenant AI endpoint credential), null = platform-plane service (shared observability collector, cross-tenant sync worker). The observer enforces `tenant_id.application_id === row.application_id` — a service account cannot scope to a tenant on a different application than its own.
